title: Sensitivity limits of non-stationary quantum sensors
abstract: The concept of the dissipative quantum limit (DQL) was first put forward in 1980s and was analyzed in detail much later in Ref. [Phys. Rev. A 103, 043721 (2021)] for the particular case of stationary (invariant with respect to a shift of time) systems. Here we extend that analysis to the general non-stationary case.
summary: This paper extends the concept of the Dissipative Quantum Limit from stationary to non-stationary quantum sensors. It demonstrates that while the DQL does not limit the sensitivity of a single known signal in non-stationary systems, it imposes a new uncertainty relation when measuring multiple signal quadratures simultaneously. This provides a deeper understanding of the fundamental limits of precision in time-varying quantum measurements.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether the Dissipative Quantum Limit (DQL), which constrains the sensitivity of stationary quantum sensors, also applies to non-stationary quantum sensors.
main_result: The author finds that for a single known signal shape, the DQL does not inherently limit sensitivity in non-stationary systems, but a DQL-like uncertainty relation emerges when attempting to simultaneously measure multiple signal quadratures.
method: The study employs linear measurement theory, commutator-based analysis, and the quantum Cramér-Rao bound to optimize noise spectral density under uncertainty constraints.
model_or_system: The model consists of a linear quantum force sensor comprising a probe subsystem and a meter subsystem, such as optomechanical sensors or gravitational-wave detectors, subject to signal, thermal, and back-action forces.
key_observables: Sum noise spectral density, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and the quadrature amplitudes of the signal force.
important_parameters: Susceptibility function (chi), dynamic rigidity (K), dissipation (friction coefficient H), and the Standard Quantum Limit (SQL) and Dissipative Quantum Limit (DQL).
main_assumptions: The sensors are assumed to be linear; the dissipation in the probe is assumed to be stationary; the meter is analyzed in a memoryless case where dynamic rigidity is zero.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of a generic linear force sensor illustrating the interaction between the probe, meter, signal force, thermal force, and back-action force.
paper_structure: The paper begins by extending the DQL analysis from stationary to non-stationary systems, moves to a mathematical framework using linear measurement theory, analyzes a specific memoryless meter case study, and concludes with the derivation of uncertainty relations for multiple signal quadratures.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems as it redefines the fundamental precision limits for time-dependent sensing protocols and explores the boundaries of the Standard Quantum Limit.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
